title: Pachidendron angustifolium
intro: Pachidendron angustifolium, also known as Philippine or Indian corkwood tree, is a tropical tree species native to Southeast Asia. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, inflammation, and respiratory issues, due to its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Pachidendron angustifolium include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant include decoctions, infusions, and extracts, often used to treat coughs, colds, and other respiratory problems.
taxonomy: Pachidendron angustifolium is a species of flowering plant in the family Annonaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the custard-apple or soursop family. This plant is native to the Philippines and is a part of the genus Pachidendron. The genus Pachidendron is a small group of flowering plants that are endemic to the Philippines. Pachidendron angustifolium is a climbing plant that produces small, white flowers. Its classification into the Annonaceae family indicates that it shares characteristics with other plants in this family, such as the production of large fruits.
